children.name: Ciconia
children.description: Large soaring birds of open farmland and wetlands. The White Stork nested on Swedish farmsteads for centuries before disappearing in the 20th century due to drainage of wet meadows; an active reintroduction programme centred on Skåne has re-established a breeding population. The Black Stork is a shy forest bird, a rare but increasing breeder.

children.name: Ciconia ciconia
children.description: The white stork is a large bird in the stork family, Ciconiidae. Its plumage is mainly white, with black on the bird's wings. Adults have long red legs and long pointed red beaks, and measure on average 100–115 cm (39–45 in) from beak tip to end of tail, with a 155–215 cm (61–85 in) wingspan.

children.name: Ciconia nigra
children.description: A shy, glossy-black forest stork that nests in tall trees near undisturbed wetlands, in contrast to its conspicuous white relative. A rare but slowly increasing breeder and migrant in Sweden.

children.name: Ciconia abdimii
children.description: The smallest stork, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa and the Sahel. Its black-and-white plumage with blue-grey bare facial skin and red knobbly legs makes it distinctive. Gregarious, nesting in large colonies on cliffs and in trees. Named after Ottoman governor El-Hadj Abdim.

children.name: Ciconia episcopus
children.description: Distinguished by its white, woolly neck and black cap. Found from sub-Saharan Africa through South and Southeast Asia. Inhabits wetlands, rice paddies, and marshes; frequently seen foraging alongside water buffalo.

children.name: Ciconia stormi
children.description: A rare and endangered stork of lowland rainforests in Sumatra, Borneo, the Malay Peninsula, and Thailand. Less than 500 individuals remain due to deforestation. Named after German ornithologist Hugo Storm. One of the most threatened stork species.

children.name: Ciconia maguari
children.description: The only Ciconia species in the Americas, found in wetlands and grasslands of eastern South America from Venezuela to Argentina. Similar to the white stork but with a black tail and red orbital skin.

children.name: Ciconia boyciana
children.description: The Oriental stork is a large, white bird with black-feathered wings in the stork family Ciconiidae.

children.name: Anastomus
children.description: Openbill storks — named for the distinctive gap (anastomosis) between the upper and lower mandibles, an adaptation for feeding on hard-shelled snails and molluscs. The curved bill tips meet only at the front, creating a pincer-like grip for extracting snails from their shells. These medium-sized storks are highly gregarious, nesting in large colonies often mixed with other waterbirds.

children.name: Anastomus oscitans
children.description: Widespread across South and Southeast Asia, from India and Sri Lanka through Myanmar and Thailand. The gap in its bill is specially adapted for extracting apple snails (Pomacea). Often seen in enormous flocks of thousands during the dry season.

children.name: Anastomus lamelligerus
children.description: Found across sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar. The bill gap is narrower than its Asian relative. Feeds primarily on large water snails. Irruptive and nomadic, following rains and snail outbreaks across the continent.

children.name: Mycteria
children.description: Wood storks and ibises — medium-to-large storks with a distinctive down-curved bill used for tactile foraging in shallow water. They feed by sweeping the bill open in murky water; when it contacts prey, the bill snaps shut in 25 milliseconds — one of the fastest reflexes in the animal kingdom. Highly colonial, often nesting alongside herons, ibises, and cormorants.

children.name: Mycteria americana
children.description: The only stork that breeds in the United States, found in Florida, Georgia, and the Carolinas. The bald, grey head and dark, naked neck are distinctive. Listed as Threatened in the US due to wetland drainage and altered hydrology.

children.name: Mycteria ibis
children.description: A striking pinkish-white stork with a bright yellow bill and orange-red facial skin, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa. Nests in mixed colonies with pelicans, herons, and cormorants. A distinctive sight on the East African savanna.

children.name: Mycteria leucocephala
children.description: A spectacular pink-and-white stork with black-and-white barred upperparts and a yellow, slightly down-curved bill. Widespread in South and Southeast Asia. Nests in large colonies on trees in freshwater wetlands. National conservation efforts in India have helped populations recover.

children.name: Mycteria cinerea
children.description: A pale, almost entirely white stork with black flight feathers and a yellow bill, found in coastal mangroves and mudflats of Southeast Asia. Endangered due to extensive habitat loss and disturbance, with fewer than 1,500 mature individuals remaining.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus
children.description: Jabirus and saddle-billed storks — very large, long-legged storks with strikingly patterned plumage and brightly coloured bills with a characteristic saddle-like shield at the base. They are among the tallest flying birds in their respective ranges. Unlike most storks, they are not highly gregarious, nesting as solitary pairs and defending large territories. Males and females have different-coloured irises — a rare trait in birds.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus
children.description: A majestic stork standing up to 137 cm tall, found in wetlands from India to Australia. The male has dark brown irises while the female has yellow. Declining across much of its range due to wetland drainage and disturbance.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus senegalensis
children.description: One of Africa's most spectacular birds, standing up to 150 cm with a wingspan of 2.7 m. Its striking red, yellow, and black bill with a yellow saddle is unmistakable. The female has yellow irises, the male brown. Inhabits open wetlands and floodplains.

children.name: Jabiru
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the jabiru of the Americas — one of the largest flying birds in the Americas, with a wingspan of up to 2.8 m. Its name comes from the Tupi-Guaraní word for 'swollen neck', referring to its inflatable throat pouch used in courtship displays.

children.name: Jabiru mycteria
children.description: A giant stork of the Pantanal, Amazon, and Chaco wetlands, standing up to 140 cm. The white body and bare black head and neck are unmistakable. Its inflatable red throat pouch expands during displays. The Pantanal population is particularly well-known and a flagship species for wetland conservation.

children.name: Leptoptilos
children.description: Adjutant storks and marabou — enormous, bare-headed storks that are among the largest flying birds in their respective ranges. Named 'adjutants' after their stiff, marching gait resembling a military officer. They are primarily scavengers, feeding on carrion, garbage, and carcasses, playing a vital sanitation role in South Asian cities. The marabou of Africa has the largest wingspan of any stork, reaching 3.2 m, yet it is one of the lightest storks thanks to its extremely light, hollow bones.

children.name: Leptoptilos crumenifer
children.description: The largest stork by wingspan (up to 3.2 m), with a bare pink head, a pendulous throat pouch, and a taste for carrion. An essential scavenger on the African savanna, often found alongside vultures. Its grey, dagger-like bill can pierce through hide.

children.name: Leptoptilos dubius
children.description: Once widespread across South and Southeast Asia, now nearly extinct with fewer than 1,200 individuals remaining in Assam and Cambodia. A massive, bald-headed scavenger standing 145 cm tall. Listed as Endangered and the subject of intensive conservation efforts in India.

children.name: Leptoptilos javanicus
children.description: Smaller than the greater adjutant but still a very large stork (110 cm), found in wetlands and forests from the Himalayas to Java and Sumatra. More solitary than its relatives, feeding primarily on fish and frogs rather than carrion.
